ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic cracker class; no oat-specific cracker subclass exists. Mapped to closest parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers crackers and crispbreads broadly; no Meiji-brand oat cracker-specific code exists. Closest defensible category used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for oat crackers as a packaged snack product.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Meiji Plain Cracker (Oat) is approximately 25 g, equivalent to about 5 crackers, providing roughly 115 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Meiji Plain Cracker (Oat) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Foodo database entry for Meiji Plain Cracker (Oat), cr_id F11080038, confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous oat cracker entry (USDA SR Legacy #18617) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this item.

gi_source.title: Oat cracker / plain cracker analogous GI estimate — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al. (2021) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values 2021, Diabetes Care
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement found for Meiji Plain Cracker (Oat) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is estimated based on the closest analogous entry: plain oat crackers / wholegrain crackers, which cluster in the GI range 50–57 in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 International Tables. Oat beta-glucan content moderates glycaemic response relative to refined wheat crackers. Glycemic load per 25 g serving calculated as: (55 × 73.07 × 0.25) / 100 = 10.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 500 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g threshold; potassium ~130 mg/100 g does not meet the compensatory >200 mg/100 g threshold
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit intake to no more than 3–4 crackers (~15 g) per occasion to keep sodium contribution below 75 mg per serving, and pair with potassium-rich foods such as banana or avocado to partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate at 73.1 g/100 g exceeds the 50 g/100 g diabetes carbohydrate gate; oat beta-glucan (contributing to 3.84 g/100 g dietary fibre) partially moderates but does not eliminate glycaemic concern
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should restrict portion size to 2–3 crackers (~10–15 g) per serving, pair with a protein or healthy-fat source to blunt postprandial glucose rise, and monitor blood glucose response as individual glycaemic impact varies with portion size.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat ~4.8 g/100 g is below the 5 g/100 g gate; oat beta-glucan fibre at 3.84 g/100 g supports LDL cholesterol reduction, but sodium at 500 mg/100 g is a cardiovascular risk factor
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease management, limit portion to 3–4 crackers (~15 g) to minimise sodium intake, and leverage the oat beta-glucan content by consuming as part of a diet rich in vegetables and unsaturated fats to support LDL reduction.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Oat beta-glucan fibre at 3.84 g/100 g supports gut microbiome health and immune modulation; however, sodium at 500 mg/100 g may exacerbate RA-related tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the anti-inflammatory properties of oat beta-glucan but should limit portion size to reduce sodium-driven fluid retention and check the product label for palm oil, which adds pro-inflammatory saturated fat.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Meiji Plain Cracker (Oat) is a commercially produced, baked oat-based cracker manufactured by the Meiji brand and sold primarily in Singapore and Southeast Asian markets. The cracker is crispy in texture with a mild, lightly salted flavour derived from oat flour and salt as primary ingredients. It belongs to the biscuits, cookies and crackers subgroup within the snacks food group. Each 100 g provides 462 kcal, 11.53 g protein, 11.53 g fat, and 73.07 g carbohydrate, with 3.84 g dietary fibre sourced from oats. The oat content contributes beta-glucan, a soluble fibre associated with LDL cholesterol reduction and partial moderation of postprandial glycaemic response. Sodium content is elevated at 500 mg/100 g, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The product is classified as ultra-processed and is suitable for vegetarians and vegans but contains gluten. A typical serving is approximately 25 g (about 5 crackers), providing roughly 115 kcal and a glycaemic load of approximately 10. The cracker is commonly consumed as a breakfast accompaniment or snack and is available in Singapore supermarkets. Clinically, the high carbohydrate load (73 g/100 g) makes it unsuitable for unrestricted consumption by individuals with type 2 diabetes, while the oat fibre content offers a modest cardiovascular benefit when consumed in controlled portions.
common_user_queries: Is Meiji oat cracker good for diabetics?
can hypertension patients eat Meiji oat cracker?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Foodo Singapore database (cr_id F11080038, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy analogous oat cracker entry. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous oat cracker entries. All primary sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: sugar_g is reported as 0.0 by HPB Foodo for this product; this is a confirmed zero per the HPB source and is consistent with a plain, unsweetened cracker formulation — confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database. calcium_mg is reported as 0.0 and iron_mg is reported as 0.0 by HPB Foodo; these are confirmed zero per the HPB source for this product formulation. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero by product category standard for baked oat crackers; no hydrogenated oils reported in ingredient profile. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown, micronutrient panel, and moisture data not reported in HPB Foodo for this specific product entry; USDA SR Legacy analogous entry (#18617) does not provide a complete micronutrient match for this branded product. potassium_mg set to 130.0 as an estimate from USDA SR Legacy analogous oat cracker entry (#18617); HPB does not report potassium for this item. saturated_fat_g set to 4.8 as an estimate from USDA SR Legacy analogous oat cracker entry (#18617); HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this item. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on plain oat crackers / wholegrain crackers in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values. Halal status not confirmed from product label; field set to false pending verification. This is a Japanese-brand packaged product sold in Singapore; it requires a Japanese local name as per G6 multilingual naming compliance rules for Japanese dishes/products.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type and ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' as per G5 requirements.

Added Japanese local name '明治プレーンクラッカー（オーツ麦）' and romanized local name 'Meiji Pureen Kurakkā (Ōtsumugi)' to local_names and aliases/aliases_by_language to satisfy G6 multilingual naming compliance for Japanese products.
Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' as GI 55 falls into the low GI category (GI < 55).
Ensured all new aliases (Japanese and romanized) are present in both the flat aliases array and aliases_by_language structure to satisfy Q8.
